source.title: Helios Defense — Private pilot check-in (signature verification issues)
source.summary: Helios reports offline install preflight is failing signature verification on the latest pilot bundle after transfer into the air-gapped network. Redwood triages live: likely bundle was modified during unzip/repack (hash mismatch) and there is also an installer version mismatch (older installer doesn’t support the detached signature layout + updated trust store). Agreed on a clean re-transfer method, validating checksums before/after copy, and upgrading the installer to the required build. Redwood to resend the bundle + provide exact commands and expected output, and Helios to share the failing logs and the install host’s system time configuration.
topics: Preflight verification failure: manifest hash mismatch vs signature invalid
Offline transfer workflow (zip/unzip tooling, AV scanning, line endings)
Installer version alignment with signed bundle format (detached manifest.sig + trust store)
Time skew / not-yet-valid signature edge case (air-gapped clock)
Support bundle collection for faster triage
next_steps: Redwood (Owen) to send Helios the exact installer version link and SHA256 for the installer binary, plus the air-gapped verification commands.
Redwood (Ethan) to confirm which pilot bundle build ID Helios should be using and resend the bundle with published checksums for the top-level tarball and manifest.
Helios (Tom/Mason) to re-transfer the bundle using a no-extract copy (scp/rsync of the original .tar.zst) and verify SHA256 before and after transfer.
Helios (Priya) to share the preflight logs + the output of the verification command (including the reported key ID / error code) via the secure upload.
Schedule a 30-min follow-up after installer upgrade and re-transfer to confirm install proceeds past preflight.
action_items: Owen Phillips (Redwood) — Email installer v0.19.3-pilot.7 download + SHA256 + GPG public key fingerprint used in pilot — Due 2025-12-03
Ethan Park (Redwood) — Re-publish bundle checksums (bundle tarball, manifest, chart tgz list) and confirm detached signature layout for build ID — Due 2025-12-03
Tom Reeves (Helios) — Re-transfer bundle without unpack/repack; run sha256sum on source + destination and send results — Due 2025-12-04
Priya Nair (Helios) — Provide system time source details (no NTP?) and current UTC time on install host; share any security gateway handling of archives — Due 2025-12-04
Rachel Kim (Redwood) — Set up follow-up call and keep pilot status tracker updated — Due 2025-12-04
source.transcript:
Meeting Header
Date: 2025-12-03
Time: 10:05 AM PT
Duration: 52 minutes
Title: Helios Defense — Private pilot check-in (signature verification issues)

Attendees
Redwood Inference: Rachel Kim, Fatima Noor, Ethan Park, Owen Phillips
Helios Defense: Tom Reeves, Priya Nair, Mason Holt, Avery Chen

Auto Summary (Fireflies)
- Customer blocked by signature verification failure during air-gapped preflight.
- Suspected causes: bundle altered during transfer/unzip; installer is older than required for signed bundle format.
- Plan: verify checksums end-to-end, re-transfer original bundle archive, upgrade installer, re-run preflight and collect logs.

Questions captured
- Does the pilot signing key rotate between builds? (No; same pilot root, but trust store updated in newer installer.)
- Can we bypass signature verification? (Break-glass exists but Redwood recommends not using during pilot except with explicit approval + audit log.)
- Is there an offline-friendly way to verify without installing? (Yes, use preflight/inspect; Redwood to send exact command.)

Transcript

[00:00] Rachel Kim: Hey everyone. Tom, Priya, Mason, Avery — thanks for joining. We saw your note that the install is failing at signature verification. We pulled Ethan and Owen in so we can debug in real time.

[00:14] Tom Reeves: Yep, thanks. We’re stuck pretty hard. We can’t get past preflight. It says signature invalid, or, uh, sometimes it says manifest mismatch. We tried twice with the same bundle.

[00:29] Priya Nair: And just to level set, our environment is fully air-gapped. We’re bringing the bundle in over approved media. Nothing can call out. We need a deterministic verification story.

[00:40] Fatima Noor: Totally. That’s exactly what this pilot is meant to validate. Can you tell us what you’re running preflight with? Like the exact command and installer version.

[00:50] Mason Holt: I can paste it. We’re using the redwood-private-installer, I think version 0.18.9? Let me check. Yeah, it prints: “Redwood Private Installer v0.18.9 (build 7c1a…)”.

[01:07] Ethan Park: Okay, that might already be a big clue. The signed offline bundle work landed in the newer pilot line. The bundle you have — does it include a detached signature file? Like manifest.sig next to manifest.json.

[01:20] Mason Holt: It has a manifest.json and also a manifest.sig. We weren’t sure if that was supposed to be embedded or not.

[01:27] Owen Phillips: Got it. The older installer expects an older layout. v0.18.x might not understand the detached signature and the trust store changes. It may mis-report it as “invalid signature” even if it’s just, like, “unknown key” or “wrong envelope.”

[01:41] Tom Reeves: That’s… frustrating but plausible. The error we saw was: “VERIFY-102: signature check failed; could not validate signing chain” and then in another run “VERIFY-221: sha mismatch for manifest.”

[01:57] Priya Nair: Our security gateway does scan and sometimes it will… repackage archives, depending on the file type. That’s one possibility.

[02:06] Rachel Kim: Okay, two vectors: installer version mismatch, and bundle modification during transfer. Let’s tackle them one at a time.

[02:15] Fatima Noor: Mason, can you read the top-level bundle filename you’re using?

[02:19] Mason Holt: “redwood-private-offline-bundle-2025.12.01-rc2-helios.tar.zst”. We copied it onto a staging jump box, then we decompressed, then moved the folder.

[02:33] Ethan Park: Ah — decompressed into a folder and then moved the folder is where we’ve seen issues. Some tools normalize line endings or change file permissions; also some orgs zip it again. The signature covers canonical bytes of the manifest (and hashes of artifacts). Any alteration will break.

[02:49] Priya Nair: We didn’t edit anything intentionally. But yes, we extracted using 7-Zip on a Windows workstation initially.

[02:56] Owen Phillips: That’s likely it. Not blaming — it’s a super common failure mode. Even if content is the same, the extracted files can end up different if it’s re-packed or if it touches JSON formatting. The manifest needs to be exactly as shipped.

[03:09] Tom Reeves: Wait, but we’re not editing the manifest. It’s just extraction.

[03:14] Owen Phillips: Right, but the installer doesn’t just verify the manifest signature. It also checks the sha256 entries inside the manifest against the actual files in the bundle directory. If any of the files got modified or truncated, it’ll say “hash mismatch.”

[03:27] Ethan Park: And if you re-zipped it or used a tool that changes symlinks or permissions, some artifact checks can fail. Depends on what we’re hashing.

[03:36] Fatima Noor: Priya, do you have a process requirement that you must extract on Windows? Or can you transfer the original .tar.zst as a binary blob and extract with Linux tar on the target host?

[03:47] Priya Nair: We can do it on Linux inside the enclave. The Windows part was just convenience for the initial move.

[03:55] Rachel Kim: Let’s align on the “safe path”:
1) Don’t unpack outside the enclave if you can avoid it.
2) Compute SHA256 of the *archive* before and after transfer.
3) Extract with standard tooling on the Linux host.
Then preflight.

[04:10] Tom Reeves: Okay. We can do that. But we still have the installer version issue.

[04:16] Ethan Park: Yes. I think you need the pilot installer build that knows about detached signatures and the trust store. I’m pretty sure it’s v0.19.3-pilot.7 for the bundle you have.

[04:28] Mason Holt: We didn’t get that note. We just used the installer from the original POC kickoff.

[04:33] Rachel Kim: That’s on us. We’ll send a single “known good” matrix: bundle build ID ↔ required installer version.

[04:42] Owen Phillips: Also, your error “VERIFY-102 could not validate signing chain” could mean the installer doesn’t have the updated public key. The newer installer includes a trust store file with multiple keys (for rollover). Older builds had only one key embedded.

[04:59] Priya Nair: When you say “trust store”, is it like a PEM bundle we can inspect?

[05:04] Owen Phillips: Yep. In the new build it’s shipped as a file in the installer resources. In air-gapped environments you can also supply it via flag. But for pilot we’re keeping it bundled so you don’t have to manage it yet.

[05:16] Tom Reeves: Our compliance folks will ask: what key is this, how do we pin it, how do we know it’s Redwood.

[05:22] Fatima Noor: We can share the fingerprint and the key ceremony doc later in the process. For the pilot we can at least provide the key ID and the fingerprint out-of-band, and you can validate it matches what installer uses.

[05:35] Ethan Park: Let’s do a quick live check: Mason, can you run the installer with “version” and also with a “preflight --verbose” and paste the first few lines? Or screen share? Up to you.

[05:46] Mason Holt: I can read it. It says:
“Installer v0.18.9
Preflight: verifying offline bundle
Found manifest: ./manifest.json
No signature file found; expecting embedded signature”
Then it fails.

[06:03] Ethan Park: Yep, there we go. That’s definitive. Your installer doesn’t support the detached signature layout.

[06:09] Tom Reeves: So it’s not that the signature is bad, it’s that the installer is too old.

[06:13] Owen Phillips: Exactly. That specific message “No signature file found” while you *do* have manifest.sig indicates it’s looking for a different name or location.

[06:22] Priya Nair: Wait, but we saw manifest.sig.

[06:24] Mason Holt: Yeah it’s definitely there.

[06:28] Ethan Park: In the newer layout it’s under bundle root next to manifest.json, but the older installer expects the signature embedded in the manifest or a different filename.

[06:38] Rachel Kim: Alright. So step one: upgrade installer.

[06:41] Tom Reeves: Okay. But what about the other error, the SHA mismatch.

[06:45] Owen Phillips: That one we should still address. After installer upgrade, if you still see hash mismatch, it’s almost certainly transfer/unpack. Let’s get you a deterministic approach.

[06:56] Fatima Noor: Tom, can you describe the transfer path? Like source machine, media, scanning step.

[07:03] Tom Reeves: Sure. Redwood sends us the bundle via secure portal. We download on an internet-connected workstation. Then we place it into our “sanitize zone” where it goes through AV scanning and content inspection. Then it gets written onto approved removable media, then into the enclave. Sometimes they require archives to be extracted and re-archived, depends on content.

[07:25] Priya Nair: It’s true. Sometimes the sanitize team will unzip because they can’t scan inside the archive. Then they zip it again.

[07:33] Ethan Park: That will break this scheme. The signature doesn’t cover the outer tarball, but the manifest references exact hashes of the inner artifacts, and the signature covers the manifest. If sanitize changes any file bytes, installer will block. That’s a feature, but we need a process that works for you.

[07:52] Priya Nair: Our process can allow binary pass-through for certain vendor-signed artifacts if we have verification on the enclave side. We’ll need to show them we can validate without unpacking outside.

[08:05] Rachel Kim: Perfect. That’s consistent with how other air-gapped teams do it. We’ll give you:
- SHA256 of the top-level archive (for transport integrity)
- The Redwood signing key fingerprint
- The preflight verification command output that proves signature OK
Then sanitize can pass it through.

[08:22] Tom Reeves: Good. That’s exactly what I need to take back.

[08:26] Owen Phillips: For the “archive checksum,” we can publish a checksum file alongside the download. For pilot we can email it too, but we prefer it in the same portal.

[08:36] Mason Holt: Once we have the new installer, what’s the command we should run?

[08:41] Ethan Park: It should be something like:
“redwood-private-installer preflight --airgapped --bundle /path/to/bundle”
And in the new build there’s also an “inspect” command, but I’m not 100% sure if it’s in pilot. Owen?

[08:54] Owen Phillips: In pilot.7 we have “bundle inspect”. It prints:
- bundle version
- build ID
- manifest digest
- signature status
- key ID
It’s super useful for support.

[09:06] Priya Nair: That’s helpful. We can include that output in our evidence.

[09:11] Rachel Kim: Great. Next, can we talk about time? Some air-gapped hosts have clock drift. We’ve seen “cert not yet valid” errors.

[09:22] Tom Reeves: Our enclave has no NTP. Time is set manually via an internal SOP. It can drift.

[09:30] Ethan Park: The signature scheme includes a validity window. If your clock is way off, it might fail with “not yet valid” or “expired.” But the error you read earlier sounded like “missing signature file,” so main issue is installer. Still, let’s confirm your system time when you re-run.

[09:48] Priya Nair: We can provide current UTC and the time source. But ideally we don’t want verification to fail because of time drift.

[09:56] Owen Phillips: Understood. There’s a mitigation we’re testing: allow a diagnostic override for time check, but it still logs loudly and we don’t want that as normal path. For now, keep your clock within, say, a day.

[10:11] Tom Reeves: That’s doable.

[10:14] Fatima Noor: Can we capture the exact bundle build ID you have? It should be in the filename and also inside the manifest.

[10:22] Mason Holt: The filename has “2025.12.01-rc2-helios”. Inside manifest it says “bundleVersion: 2025.12.01-rc2” and “buildId: rbw-priv-6f12d9”.

[10:36] Ethan Park: Great. That build ID matches what I have in my notes. The installer should be v0.19.3-pilot.7 or later.

[10:45] Rachel Kim: Owen, can you commit to sending that installer binary and the checksum today?

[10:50] Owen Phillips: Yes. I’ll send the download link, sha256, and the public key fingerprint we’re using. Also the exact commands with expected success output.

[11:01] Tom Reeves: We’ll need it in a format we can bring in. Link is fine; we’ll download on the outside.

[11:07] Priya Nair: And we’ll need the checksum out-of-band, ideally on letterhead, but email from Redwood is okay for pilot.

[11:14] Rachel Kim: Noted. For GA we’ll have a formal doc.

[11:18] Fatima Noor: Once you re-transfer the bundle, can you run sha256sum on the archive on both sides and send us the two values? That will tell us if the sanitize step modified it.

[11:29] Mason Holt: Yes, that’s easy.

[11:32] Tom Reeves: Actually, can we avoid extraction entirely? Like point installer at the archive file.

[11:38] Ethan Park: Not yet. The current installer expects a directory layout after extraction. It’s on our roadmap to support verifying the archive directly, but for this pilot we’re not there.

[11:52] Tom Reeves: Understood.

[11:54] Priya Nair: The sanitize process sometimes insists on extracting. If we can show them the on-enclave verification, they can approve pass-through.

[12:03] Rachel Kim: Right. We’ll give you the language: “Do not unpack or repackage; treat as vendor-signed artifact; verify inside enclave with Redwood installer preflight.”

[12:14] Owen Phillips: Also, if you end up with a directory already, but the files were re-zipped, you can still test integrity by running “bundle inspect” and it will tell you which file hash mismatched. That’s useful for pinpointing.

[12:29] Mason Holt: That’s great because we suspected one of the chart tgz files got truncated.

[12:35] Ethan Park: That’s consistent with “hash mismatch.” It could be the Helm charts tarballs, container image index file, or the SBOM pointer file.

[12:44] Priya Nair: SBOM pointer — what is that in the bundle.

[12:48] Ethan Park: It’s a file in the bundle that references the SBOM artifact (or includes it) and includes its hash. The manifest lists it so we can validate offline.

[12:58] Priya Nair: Good. That’s important for us.

[13:02] Rachel Kim: Let’s switch to what you need to get unblocked today. Tom, what is your internal timeline.

[13:09] Tom Reeves: We’re supposed to have the pilot environment installed by end of week for the internal security review. So basically we need it working by Thursday.

[13:19] Rachel Kim: Okay. We can support that.

[13:22] Fatima Noor: And we should avoid break-glass. Just to be explicit: there is an override flag in newer builds, but it produces audit warnings and we don’t want to normalize it.

[13:33] Priya Nair: Agreed. If we use an override, our CISO will consider the pilot invalid.

[13:38] Owen Phillips: Yep.

[13:40] Ethan Park: Once you upgrade installer, the first thing you should see in output is something like:
“Trusted signing keys: 2”
“Signature: valid (keyId …)”
If you see “unknown key,” that means trust store wasn’t updated.

[13:56] Mason Holt: Got it.

[14:00] Tom Reeves: How does key rotation work, by the way.

[14:03] Rachel Kim: For pilot, we don’t rotate. For production we’ll have a rotation policy and the installer can trust multiple keys so you can update trust roots without bricking installs. We can cover that in the next call if helpful.

[14:16] Priya Nair: Yes, we’ll need it eventually.

[14:18] Fatima Noor: We also want to capture logs. When preflight fails, where do the logs go.

[14:26] Owen Phillips: In the new build it prints the log path and also includes verification metadata in the support bundle. For now, just grab:
- the preflight output
- the log file path output
- and run “support-bundle create” if it’s available.

[14:44] Mason Holt: Okay.

[14:46] (crosstalk)
Tom Reeves: —we can do that.
Priya Nair: —make sure it doesn’t include sensitive stuff.

[14:50] Owen Phillips: It’s mostly operational metadata; you can review before sharing. If needed we can do a redacted bundle.

[14:58] Rachel Kim: Alright, let’s do a quick checklist.

[15:02] Rachel Kim: Item 1: Owen sends installer v0.19.3-pilot.7 and checksums today.

[15:07] Owen Phillips: Yes.

[15:09] Rachel Kim: Item 2: Ethan resends bundle checksums and confirms the exact layout expected.

[15:14] Ethan Park: Yes, I’ll verify the buildId rbw-priv-6f12d9 outputs and publish sha256 list.

[15:22] Rachel Kim: Item 3: Helios re-transfers the *original* tar.zst file without unpack/repack, validates sha before/after.

[15:30] Tom Reeves: Yes. I’ll coordinate with sanitize.

[15:34] Priya Nair: And I’ll provide the process exception request with the justification and the verification evidence.

[15:40] Rachel Kim: Great.

[15:42] Fatima Noor: While we’re here, can you share the exact text of the hash mismatch error you saw earlier? That can help us target.

[15:50] Mason Holt: It said: “VERIFY-221: digest mismatch for artifacts/charts/redwood-core-0.7.12.tgz expected 9d… got 3f…” It was long.

[16:07] Ethan Park: That’s a chart tgz, so it definitely got changed or truncated. If sanitize extracted and re-zipped, it may have produced a different tgz even if the chart contents are same.

[16:19] Priya Nair: That’s exactly what they do.

[16:22] Owen Phillips: Then we should stop them from re-tarring. The tgz bytes must match.

[16:28] Tom Reeves: Understood. We’ll push for binary pass-through.

[16:32] Rachel Kim: Anything else blocking.

[16:35] Avery Chen: Just from program side, we need a crisp statement we can put in the pilot report: what happened, what the fix is.

[16:42] Rachel Kim: We’ll send a written recap: “Installer version mismatch + sanitize re-archive broke hashes; fix is upgrade installer and transfer original archive unchanged; verify with preflight.”

[16:55] Avery Chen: Perfect.

[16:57] Priya Nair: One more question: If we have to keep the bundle for months, does signature expire.

[17:03] Ethan Park: The signature has a validity window, but it’s long enough for typical offline retention. If you keep it for, like, years, we’d recommend updating to a newer signed bundle anyway. For pilot, you’re fine.

[17:16] Priya Nair: Okay.

[17:18] Tom Reeves: Also, can we pin to a specific key, like we don’t want surprises.

[17:24] Owen Phillips: Yes, in newer builds you can view the key fingerprint used for validation. We can also provide the fingerprint you should expect. For GA there will be documentation on trust root updates.

[17:36] Fatima Noor: We’ll cover it. For now, get you installed.

[17:40] Rachel Kim: Cool. Let’s schedule a follow-up for tomorrow afternoon? After you’ve done installer upgrade + re-transfer.

[17:48] Tom Reeves: Works.

[17:50] Priya Nair: Yes.

[17:52] Rachel Kim: Great. We’ll send the invite. Any last questions.

[17:58] Mason Holt: None.

[18:00] Rachel Kim: Alright, thanks all. Owen and Ethan will follow up by email today with links, checksums, and commands.

[18:08] (end of transcript; remainder of meeting included troubleshooting chatter and scheduling but no new technical content)

title: Helios Defense — Private pilot check-in (signature verification issues)
preview: Helios reports offline install preflight is failing signature verification on the latest pilot bundle after transfer into the air-gapped network. Redwood triages live: likely bundle was modified during unzip/repack (hash mismatch) and there is also an installer version mismatch (older installer doesn’t support the detached signature layout + updated trust store). Agreed on a clean re-transfer method, validating checksums before/after copy, and upgrading the installer to the required build. Redwood to resend the bundle + provide exact commands and expected output, and Helios to share the failing logs and the install host’s system time configuration.

Redwood triages live: likely bundle was modified during unzip/repack (hash mismatch) and there is also an installer version mismatch (older installer doesn’t support the detached signature layout + updated trust store). source.title: Helios Defense - Air-gapped Redwood Private deployment deep dive (offline bundles + verification)
source.summary: Helios Defense walked through their air-gapped environment constraints (no outbound network, controlled inbound media, strict change control, limited time sync) and asked for an end-to-end, offline-verifiable install/upgrade flow for Redwood Private. Redwood outlined the proposed offline bundle format (bundle + manifest + detached signature), trust-root handling, and installer preflight verification that fails closed on mismatch. Helios clarified acceptance criteria: deterministic artifact list, offline integrity verification steps they can run independently, clear audit evidence on install, and an approved break-glass path with logging. Open questions centered on key rotation in air-gapped sites, handling time skew for signature validity, and how downgrade/replay protection will work with a local install state.
topics: Offline bundle contents and manifest hashing
Signature verification flow during installer preflight
Trust roots / public key distribution and rotation
questions: Can we verify everything offline without contacting Redwood, including Helm charts and image references?
How do we update trusted signing keys in an air-gapped enclave during key rotation?
What happens if the system clock is wrong (no NTP) and signatures appear not-yet-valid?
How do you prevent downgrade/replay if someone reuses an old signed bundle?
What evidence artifacts can we attach to our change ticket / ATO package?
action_items: Redwood (Colin) to send Helios the draft offline verification command list (bundle inspect + signature verify + manifest hash check) and expected outputs.
Redwood (Marcus) to share a short write-up for Helios on key custody model (Redwood-managed signing keys, protected pipeline, audit logs) and planned key rotation procedure for air-gapped customers.
Helios (Mike) to confirm which crypto libraries/algorithms are approved in their environment (Ed25519 vs ECDSA P-256, SHA-256 requirements, FIPS constraints).
Helios (Priya) to provide their Kubernetes version + container runtime details (containerd, registry mirroring approach) and whether they can run a small preflight binary on a bastion host.
Redwood (Bruno) to propose a pilot install runbook template tailored to Helios media transfer and change-control process.
next_steps: Schedule a 60-minute follow-up focused on key rotation + trust store update flow in air-gapped sites.
Redwood to provide an example signed bundle layout and sample manifest (sanitized) for Helios to review internally.
Helios to identify required audit log fields for their ATO evidence (who/when/what bundle ID, verification result, break-glass usage).
source.transcript:
Meeting header
Date: 2025-10-14
Start time: 9:00 AM PT
Duration: 57 min
Title: Helios Defense - Air-gapped Redwood Private deployment deep dive (offline bundles + verification)
Attendees (Redwood): Fatima Noor, Colin O'Donnell, Bruno Silva, Marcus Lin
Attendees (Helios Defense): Rachel Hayes, Mike Stanton, Priya Nair, Tomás Alvarez

Auto-summary (Fireflies)
- Helios requested an offline, cryptographically verifiable install/upgrade process for Redwood Private in an air-gapped enclave.
- Redwood explained bundle manifest + detached signature approach and installer preflight verification.
- Discussed constraints: time sync, key rotation, downgrade protection, audit evidence, and break-glass.


[00:00] Fatima Noor: Alright, cool. Thanks everybody for joining. This is the deep dive on air-gapped deployment for Redwood Private, specifically the offline bundles and how verification would work. We’ve got Marcus from Security here too.

[00:13] Rachel Hayes: Great, thanks Fatima. Yeah we want to go pretty nuts-and-bolts today. Our environment is… it’s not “mostly offline,” it’s like, literally no egress. So we need to be able to prove integrity without calling anything out.

[00:27] Colin O'Donnell: Yep. Understood. We’ve been collecting those requirements across a few regulated customers. We can walk through the proposed artifact bundle format and the preflight verify flow.

[00:39] Mike Stanton: And also what “proof” we can keep. Like audit artifacts for our ATO package.

[00:44] Bruno Silva: Yep, and we can cover operational runbook style, what the installer logs, what you can export.

[00:52] Fatima Noor: Sounds good. Maybe Rachel, can you give a quick “here’s our constraints” recap, just to ground it?

[01:00] Rachel Hayes: Sure. So: air-gapped enclave, no outbound DNS, no outbound HTTPS, nothing. Inbound is via approved media only, scanned. We have a bastion that can run CLI tools but that bastion also has no egress. Time sync is… tricky. We have an internal NTP, but it’s not always reliable and sometimes we see drift.

[01:24] Priya Nair: And Kubernetes is on-prem, containerd, private registry. We mirror images into that registry manually. Helm charts we usually vendor, like tarballs. We do not allow “helm repo add” to external.

[01:37] Tomás Alvarez: Also change control: every upgrade requires a ticket, evidence attached, and we need a documented “what verifies what.”

[01:45] Mike Stanton: Crypto: we are FIPS-ish. Not everything needs to be FIPS 140-2 validated, but anything that’s security critical gets scrutiny. Hashing should be SHA-256, signature algorithm we need to confirm, but avoid anything exotic.

[02:02] Fatima Noor: Perfect. So, Redwood objective: ship an offline bundle that includes everything needed to install or upgrade Private, and that bundle is signed by Redwood. The installer verifies it and blocks if it’s tampered.

[02:16] Colin O'Donnell: Concretely, we’re proposing: a bundle directory (or tarball), containing: (1) the installer payload, (2) Helm chart artifacts and values schemas as needed, (3) a manifest file listing every artifact and its hash, plus version/build metadata, (4) a signature over the manifest.

[02:36] Priya Nair: When you say “every artifact,” does that include container images? Because images are huge.

[02:43] Colin O'Donnell: Great point. We don’t sign the binary blobs of images inside the bundle in v1, because we’re not shipping a full OCI layout in the same tar necessarily. What we do: the manifest contains image references (repo/name:tag or digest), and for offline installs we strongly recommend digest pinning. We can also include a file with the digest list that you can validate against your mirrored registry.

[03:05] Mike Stanton: I’m going to push on that. Tags are mutable. If it’s tag-based, it’s not acceptable.

[03:10] Colin O'Donnell: Totally agree. The acceptance baseline is digest pinning. Tags can exist for human readability but the installer will use digests for pull.

[03:19] Bruno Silva: And we can run a preflight that checks “are these digests present in your registry” before you actually roll.

[03:26] Rachel Hayes: Okay. For the charts, you said tarballs. We can handle that. The manifest would include sha256 of each chart tarball?

[03:34] Colin O'Donnell: Exactly. sha256 or sha512, but sha256 is typical. The manifest is the “single source of truth.” Anything installed references something listed in the manifest and the hash must match.

[03:49] Marcus Lin: From the security side, the goal is: you verify a signature over a canonicalized manifest. Then you hash-check the artifacts on disk against what the manifest says. That gives integrity and provenance, assuming the trust root is managed.

[04:06] Tomás Alvarez: “Canonicalized” meaning what?

[04:10] Colin O'Donnell: Meaning we don’t want signature instability due to JSON ordering differences. So we’ll define deterministic serialization rules. In practice it’s: stable key ordering, UTF-8, no floats, normalized newlines, stuff like that.

[04:28] Priya Nair: Is the manifest JSON or YAML?

[04:31] Colin O'Donnell: Leaning JSON for canonicalization. YAML is flexible but harder to canonicalize. But we can ship a human-friendly “summary” file too.

[04:42] Mike Stanton: And the signature itself, is that like embedded in the manifest or detached?

[04:47] Colin O'Donnell: Detached. So manifest.json and manifest.sig. Keeps the manifest readable and avoids accidental edits.

[04:55] Rachel Hayes: Okay. What algorithm? We’ve seen Ed25519 and ECDSA P-256. Which one are you standardizing on?

[05:04] Marcus Lin: We’re still finalizing with our internal standard, but we’re optimizing for wide support and “no surprises” in offline environments. ECDSA P-256 is generally supported in FIPS contexts. Ed25519 is great but sometimes gets pushback in strict FIPS. We can follow up once it’s locked.

[05:26] Mike Stanton: Please. Because if you land on Ed25519 and my scanners hate it, we’ll have a fight.

[05:31] Fatima Noor: Noted. We’ll treat that as a gating requirement for Helios.

[05:37] Colin O'Donnell: Regardless of algorithm: you’ll have a Redwood public key (or cert) distributed with the installer trust store. The installer verifies manifest.sig against manifest.json and that trusted key.

[05:54] Priya Nair: How do we update that trusted key? Because if it’s embedded in the installer and the installer is in the bundle… chicken-and-egg.

[06:03] Colin O'Donnell: Yeah, this is one of the core design points. We’re planning a trust store that can include multiple keys, and keys can be rotated with overlap. For air-gapped customers, we can ship a trust-store update file as part of a prior release, so your installer can accept both old and new keys for a window.

[06:25] Marcus Lin: Plus a documented key ceremony and rotation cadence. From your perspective: you’d be told “new key is active starting release X, update trust store before then.”

[06:36] Tomás Alvarez: That implies you publish the public keys out-of-band too.

[06:40] Marcus Lin: Correct. We can provide fingerprints via multiple channels: docs, release notes, and in the signed bundle itself. But the “first trust” has to be established once—typically the initial installer includes the trust root.

[06:56] Mike Stanton: Establishing first trust: we’ll need to validate that key fingerprint through our procurement and security chain. So give us a stable fingerprint and a statement of how it’s protected.

[07:07] Marcus Lin: Yep. We can provide a signing key custody statement: stored in HSM-backed KMS, access controlled, dual approvals in CI, audit logs. We’ll send a short write-up.

[07:21] Rachel Hayes: Let’s talk about verification steps. What exactly happens during install?

[07:26] Bruno Silva: Our installer has a “preflight” mode. In air-gapped mode, it will not attempt to fetch anything. Preflight would: (1) locate the bundle path, (2) load manifest.json, (3) verify manifest.sig using the local trust store, (4) validate bundle version metadata and build ID, (5) hash-check each included artifact, (6) optionally check that required images by digest exist in your mirrored registry.

[07:54] Priya Nair: Hash-checking each artifact might be slow. We’ve got big files.

[07:59] Bruno Silva: It will add time, yes. But we can scope what’s “required” to hash. For example, binaries, chart tarballs, config templates, SBOM pointer file. For large model artifacts, we may do staged verification or allow pre-verification with a separate command.

[08:17] Colin O'Donnell: Also we can keep the manifest to cover file sizes and only hash what’s in the bundle. If you’re separately transferring model weights, those can be covered too, but that’s a larger bundle.

[08:30] Mike Stanton: For us, model weights are often separate, but the runtime and installer bits are critical.

[08:36] Fatima Noor: Helios acceptance criteria—what’s “must have” for phase one?

[08:41] Rachel Hayes: Must have: (a) offline verification without external calls, (b) deterministic list of artifacts with hashes, (c) signature verification in the installer that fails closed, (d) clear logging we can attach to our change ticket.

[08:58] Tomás Alvarez: Also if something fails verification, the error message needs to tell us what file failed and why. We can’t debug “signature invalid” with no detail.

[09:07] Bruno Silva: Yep. We’re building an error taxonomy. Like: UNKNOWN_KEY, SIGNATURE_INVALID, MANIFEST_HASH_MISMATCH, FILE_MISSING, FILE_HASH_MISMATCH, VERSION_DOWNGRADE_BLOCKED, and also TIME_INVALID if cert not yet valid.

[09:26] Mike Stanton: Time. Let’s dig into time. If our system clock is off, signatures that have “not before” might fail.

[09:33] Marcus Lin: Right. So we can either (1) avoid time-bounded cert validity for the signature itself and just use raw public-key signatures, or (2) if using X.509, keep a long validity and provide guidance for time sync. The simplest for air-gapped is: signature verification does not depend on current time, but we still want revocation/rotation story.

[09:59] Rachel Hayes: If you don’t have time checks, how do you handle revoked keys?

[10:03] Marcus Lin: In air-gapped, you can’t do online revocation checks. So the model is: trust store updates are your revocation mechanism. If a key is compromised, we publish an updated trust store that removes it, and you update your environment.

[10:18] Mike Stanton: That’s acceptable if you have a process. But we need that documented.

[10:23] Fatima Noor: We will. There’s going to be a key rotation and emergency rotation runbook.

[10:30] Priya Nair: Downgrade prevention. What stops someone from installing an older bundle that’s still validly signed?

[10:38] Colin O'Donnell: Two layers. First: the manifest includes a bundle version and build ID. Second: the installer writes a local “install state” record with the highest installed version/build. Then on install/upgrade it blocks if bundle version is lower than installed, unless you explicitly override for rollback.

[10:59] Rachel Hayes: We do rollbacks sometimes. But it’s controlled.

[11:03] Bruno Silva: Exactly. Rollback can be allowed if it’s a “known rollback” path and you use a separate flag. And that event is logged as a rollback, distinct from bypass.

[11:16] Mike Stanton: And replay? Like same version but different contents.

[11:20] Colin O'Donnell: That’s what hashes catch. If contents differ, hash mismatch. If someone tries to repackage with same manifest but swapped files, hash mismatch. If they change manifest, signature invalid.

[11:35] Tomás Alvarez: What about someone copying a signed manifest and signature from one bundle and applying it to another.

[11:41] Colin O'Donnell: Doesn’t work because signature is over the manifest content and the manifest includes the hashes. Unless the other bundle matches exactly, it will fail.

[11:51] Priya Nair: Okay.

[11:53] Fatima Noor: Can we walk through what the operator actually runs? Like commands.

[11:58] Bruno Silva: Sure. Example:
1) `redwood-private-installer bundle inspect --path /mnt/media/redwood-private-1.8.0-bundle` (prints versions, build id, lists artifacts)
2) `redwood-private-installer bundle verify --path ...` (verifies signature + hashes)
3) `redwood-private-installer preflight --airgapped --bundle-path ... --registry https://registry.helio.local` (optional image presence check)
4) `redwood-private-installer install --airgapped --bundle-path ...`

[12:30] Rachel Hayes: The verify output—can it be machine readable? We sometimes attach JSON to tickets.

[12:36] Bruno Silva: Yes. We can do `--output json` but I’m not sure if that’s in v1. At minimum it prints a stable summary plus log files.

[12:48] Mike Stanton: I’d prefer JSON. Even if it’s just “bundle_id, signature_key_fingerprint, verified:true, timestamp.”

[12:55] Bruno Silva: Noted.

[12:58] Marcus Lin: On audit evidence: we can emit an audit event locally like `bundle_verification_success` with bundle ID and key ID. If you have a SIEM exporter, you can forward. In air-gapped, you can retain locally and export via file.

[13:17] Tomás Alvarez: For ATO we need: who ran it and when.

[13:21] Rachel Hayes: Yeah, user identity.

[13:23] Bruno Silva: The installer can capture effective user, hostname, and the command line flags. For identity, if it’s run via a CI runner or privileged account, it’ll log that. We can’t do SSO in an enclave unless you wire it, but we can log OS user.

[13:41] Mike Stanton: Careful logging command line, might contain secrets.

[13:45] Bruno Silva: Good point. We’ll redact sensitive flags, and we can explicitly not log values. We can log presence of flags and non-secret identifiers.

[13:55] Priya Nair: On SBOM, do you include an SBOM in the bundle?

[14:00] Marcus Lin: We can include a pointer and hash to an SBOM artifact. Some customers want the SBOM file itself in the bundle. We can do either. The manifest would include the SBOM filename and sha.

[14:15] Mike Stanton: For Helios, we likely need the SBOM included, not a URL pointer.

[14:20] Fatima Noor: That’s doable. Let’s capture that. (SBOM included in bundle.)

[14:27] Colin O'Donnell: The bundle can include `sbom.spdx.json` or similar. Then verify hashes.

[14:35] Rachel Hayes: How big will these bundles be realistically.

[14:39] Bruno Silva: Without model weights and without images, it’s manageable—hundreds of MB maybe a couple GB depending on charts and runtime. If we include images, it becomes tens of GB.

[14:54] Priya Nair: We don’t want images from you. We mirror from our pipeline. We just want a digest list.

[14:59] Colin O'Donnell: Great. That’s consistent.

[15:02] Mike Stanton: Okay so acceptance: bundle includes charts tarballs + checksums, installer binary, manifest, signature, SBOM, and image digest list.

[15:12] Fatima Noor: Yep.

[15:15] Rachel Hayes: Key custody. Marcus you mentioned HSM-backed. Is it like a human signs it or CI signs.

[15:21] Marcus Lin: CI signs, but with protected keys. Access is constrained; we require approvals for the signing step, and we log who approved. There’s a key ceremony for rotations.

[15:36] Tomás Alvarez: We’ll want that spelled out for procurement.

[15:40] Marcus Lin: Understood.

[15:42] Priya Nair: Failure modes. If verification fails, the installer stops before doing anything? Or can it partially install.

[15:49] Bruno Silva: Fail closed. Verification happens in preflight and install begins only after it passes. We also verify again right before applying changes.

[16:01] Mike Stanton: Good.

[16:03] Rachel Hayes: What about break-glass? We have “emergency” procedures but they’re heavily audited.

[16:09] Fatima Noor: So we’re planning a break-glass override flag, but it’s policy gated. The idea is: by default it’s disabled or requires a config file to enable. When used, it emits loud warnings and an audit event. It should not be used for normal operations.

[16:30] Marcus Lin: From security: break-glass should require explicit documented approval on your side and ours in extreme cases, and we’ll provide an exception process. It will be very clear that it’s not recommended.

[16:43] Mike Stanton: I’m okay with it existing as long as it’s not silent and it’s not easy.

[16:48] Priya Nair: And it shouldn’t just continue to download things.

[16:52] Bruno Silva: Right. In air-gapped mode, downloads are disabled entirely. If a file is missing, it fails and tells you what’s missing.

[17:04] Rachel Hayes: Okay.

[17:06] Fatima Noor: Can we talk about “what exactly is signed”? Like is it just the manifest or each file.

[17:11] Colin O'Donnell: Signature is over the manifest. Manifest contains hashes of each file. That’s the standard pattern. Signing each file individually is heavier and not necessary.

[17:23] Tomás Alvarez: Provided the manifest is complete.

[17:25] Colin O'Donnell: Exactly. Completeness is key. We’ll make “no extra files” either ignored or flagged depending on mode. Typically, extra files are allowed but not trusted; required files must be present and match hash.

[17:41] Mike Stanton: I would prefer “extra files” triggers a warning. Because a malicious actor could tuck something in and hope it gets used.

[17:49] Bruno Silva: The installer won’t use anything not referenced in the manifest. But yes, we can warn on unknown files.

[18:00] Priya Nair: Also, for Windows paths—we do have some Windows jump boxes. But install runs on Linux.

[18:07] Colin O'Donnell: We’ve seen customers with weird path separators. We’ll ensure manifest paths are normalized and the tool can handle spaces.

[18:18] Rachel Hayes: Another acceptance: We need to be able to independently verify without the installer. Like with openssl.

[18:26] Marcus Lin: That’s possible. If we use common formats, you can run `openssl dgst -verify` or equivalent. If we use a custom envelope, we’ll provide a small offline verifier binary. We’ll likely aim for standard tooling compatibility.

[18:43] Mike Stanton: Please aim for standard tooling. It helps audits.

[18:47] Colin O'Donnell: Understood.

[18:50] Fatima Noor: Let’s see if any other constraints we haven’t covered.

[18:54] Priya Nair: Logging location. Where do logs go? We need to harvest them.

[18:58] Bruno Silva: Installer writes logs to `/var/log/redwood-private-installer/` by default, plus a summarized “verification report” you can save next to the bundle. We can also include verification status in the support bundle.

[19:14] Rachel Hayes: Support bundle meaning if we open a ticket with you.

[19:17] Bruno Silva: Exactly. It includes the manifest metadata, key fingerprint used, and any error codes. No secrets.

[19:28] Mike Stanton: Can the audit events be written in JSON lines.

[19:31] Bruno Silva: Yes, JSONL is typical. (We’ll confirm.)

[19:37] Tomás Alvarez: One more: acceptance criteria for pilot. We need a list we can sign off.

[19:43] Fatima Noor: We can propose a pilot acceptance checklist and iterate.

[19:48] Colin O'Donnell: I’ll capture what we heard:
- Offline bundle includes installer, charts, digest list, SBOM
- Manifest with hashes and build ID
- Detached signature, standard verifiable format
- Installer preflight verifies signature and hashes, fails closed
- Clear error messages and audit log output
- Key rotation plan and trust store update mechanism
- Downgrade prevention with controlled rollback

[20:21] Rachel Hayes: That’s basically it.

[20:24] Mike Stanton: And algorithm needs to be compatible. Prefer P-256 with SHA-256, but we’ll confirm.

[20:31] Marcus Lin: We’ll follow up on algorithm selection.

[20:34] Priya Nair: For registry digest check: can it just read from a file of “images present” rather than pinging the registry. Because some of our registries don’t allow listing.

[20:45] Bruno Silva: That’s a good point. We can do a mode that checks only local `crane` outputs or a precomputed inventory file. Or we can do “attempt to pull manifest by digest” which is low-impact.

[21:01] Priya Nair: Pulling might be blocked too.

[21:03] Bruno Silva: Okay, then file-based check is better.

[21:08] Fatima Noor: We can treat that as a follow-up design detail.

[21:12] Rachel Hayes: Another question: if we’re installing from bundle, do we need to run anything on the cluster before verifying?

[21:19] Colin O'Donnell: No. Verification is on the host running installer. It’s all filesystem-based plus optional registry check.

[21:29] Tomás Alvarez: Great.

[21:31] (crosstalk)
Mike Stanton: Sorry—one more, provenance. Can you provide SLSA attestation or something.

[21:39] Marcus Lin: We’re working on build provenance. In the short term, you’ll have signed manifests and release metadata. Longer term, yes, we can provide provenance artifacts, but in air-gapped we need an offline consumable format.

[21:56] Mike Stanton: Understood. Not a blocker for pilot.

[22:00] Fatima Noor: Okay. Maybe we shift into “walkthrough example.” Colin, can you describe a sample bundle layout.

[22:06] Colin O'Donnell: Sure. Something like:
- `/bundle/manifest.json`
- `/bundle/manifest.sig`
- `/bundle/sbom.spdx.json`
- `/bundle/charts/redwood-private-1.8.0.tgz`
- `/bundle/charts/checksums.txt` (though checksums are also in manifest)
- `/bundle/images/images.lock` (digest list)
- `/bundle/installer/redwood-private-installer` (binary)
- `/bundle/README.txt` (human instructions)

[22:39] Rachel Hayes: Nice.

[22:41] Priya Nair: Is the installer itself covered by the manifest hash.

[22:45] Colin O'Donnell: Yes. Installer binary hash in manifest.

[22:49] Mike Stanton: Good.

[22:51] Tomás Alvarez: For audit evidence, can we store the manifest and signature as an attachment and it’s meaningful.

[22:57] Marcus Lin: Yes. You can keep the manifest + signature + verification output + bundle build ID. That’s a solid evidence set.

[23:09] Fatima Noor: Great. Let’s talk about how this aligns with your internal change control. Do you require two-person review for installs.

[23:16] Rachel Hayes: Yes, typically. One person runs commands, another reviews evidence. Sometimes we record terminal output.

[23:25] Bruno Silva: Then we’ll make sure outputs are stable and “copy-pasteable.”

[23:31] Mike Stanton: Also in our enclave, we don’t allow interactive prompts in automation. The installer should be non-interactive.

[23:39] Bruno Silva: We support `--yes` style flags and config files. For air-gapped, we can keep it deterministic.

[23:48] Priya Nair: Cool.

[23:50] Fatima Noor: Any concerns with manifest size.

[23:54] Priya Nair: If it lists thousands of files, maybe. But if it’s just the bundle artifacts, not images, it’s fine.

[24:03] Colin O'Donnell: We’re not listing per-image layer files. Just digest list.

[24:08] Mike Stanton: Okay.

[24:10] (pause)

[24:12] Fatima Noor: I want to make sure we address “unknown key.” Suppose you get a bundle signed by a key your installer doesn’t trust. What’s desired behavior.

[24:21] Mike Stanton: Block. Always.

[24:24] Rachel Hayes: Block, but with good message: “update trust store.”

[24:29] Bruno Silva: Great. That’s the plan: unknown key blocks and points to steps.

[24:37] Tomás Alvarez: And that message should reference the key ID/fingerprint so we can compare to release notes.

[24:44] Bruno Silva: Yep.

[24:47] Priya Nair: Okay.

[24:49] Fatima Noor: We’re at ~25 minutes. Let’s see if we can cover the pilot timeline quickly. Rachel, when do you want to do a pilot install.

[24:57] Rachel Hayes: November-ish. We need to complete internal review by early Nov, then schedule a maintenance window.

[25:06] Fatima Noor: That lines up with our Phase 0/1 work. We can probably provide a draft bundle spec and sample outputs in the next two weeks.

[25:16] Mike Stanton: If you can send the key custody statement and algorithm choice soon, that helps.

[25:22] Marcus Lin: Got it.

[25:25] Priya Nair: Question: if we have multiple clusters, can the same bundle be used.

[25:30] Bruno Silva: Yes, the bundle is cluster-agnostic. Config values differ but artifacts same.

[25:37] Rachel Hayes: Perfect.

[25:39] Mike Stanton: One more security thing: do you sign the “values” file too.

[25:44] Colin O'Donnell: Customer-provided values are not signed by Redwood. But the installer can record a hash of the values file used, for audit traceability.

[25:56] Tomás Alvarez: That would be good for evidence.

[25:59] Bruno Silva: We can log “values file sha256” in audit events.

[26:05] Mike Stanton: Nice.

[26:07] Fatima Noor: That’s a good point. We’ll add it.

[26:12] (brief crosstalk)

[26:14] Priya Nair: Sorry, does the manifest include Kubernetes manifests too, like YAML templates.

[26:19] Colin O'Donnell: If they are part of the bundle, yes. Typically charts encapsulate that.

[26:26] Priya Nair: Okay.

[26:28] Fatima Noor: Anything else on acceptance criteria.

[26:31] Rachel Hayes: I think we covered the big stuff.

[26:34] Mike Stanton: I want to make sure break-glass is not a hidden “install anyway.” It needs to be explicit.

[26:40] Bruno Silva: Agreed.

[26:42] Marcus Lin: And policy documented.

[26:45] Fatima Noor: Great. Let’s shift to recap and next steps.

[26:50] Fatima Noor: Redwood will send (1) draft verification command list and expected outputs, (2) key custody statement and algorithm update, (3) example bundle layout and sample manifest fields (sanitized), and (4) a pilot runbook template. Helios will send back crypto constraints and K8s/runtime details.

[27:14] Rachel Hayes: Sounds good.

[27:16] Priya Nair: Yes.

[27:18] Mike Stanton: Yep.

[27:20] Tomás Alvarez: Thank you.

[27:23] Fatima Noor: Before we close, do you want to schedule the follow-up on key rotation/trust store.

[27:29] Rachel Hayes: Yes, let’s do next week. Same group.

[27:33] Fatima Noor: Perfect, I’ll send options.

[27:36] Meeting ended (recording continued with small talk)

[27:40] (small talk)
Colin O'Donnell: Priya, you said containerd, what version.
Priya Nair: I’ll email you, I don’t remember offhand.

[28:05] End of transcript.

title: Helios Defense - Air-gapped Redwood Private deployment deep dive (offline bundles + verification)
preview: Helios Defense walked through their air-gapped environment constraints (no outbound network, controlled inbound media, strict change control, limited time sync) and asked for an end-to-end, offline-verifiable install/upgrade flow for Redwood Private. Redwood outlined the proposed offline bundle format (bundle + manifest + detached signature), trust-root handling, and installer preflight verification that fails closed on mismatch. Helios clarified acceptance criteria: deterministic artifact list, offline integrity verification steps they can run independently, clear audit evidence on install, and an approved break-glass path with logging. Open questions centered on key rotation in air-gapped sites, handling time skew for signature validity, and how downgrade/replay protection will work with a local install state.
text: Helios Defense walked through their air-gapped environment constraints (no outbound network, controlled inbound media, strict change control, limited time sync) and asked for an end-to-end, offline-verifiable install/upgrade flow for Redwood Private. Redwood outlined the proposed offline bundle format (bundle + manifest + detached signature), trust-root handling, and installer preflight verification that fails closed on mismatch. Helios clarified acceptance criteria: deterministic artifact list, offline integrity verification steps they can run independently, clear audit evidence on install, and an approved break-glass path with logging. Open questions centered on key rotation in air-gapped sites, handling time skew for signature validity, and how downgrade/replay protection will work with a local install state.
